C2_reason: Uses ABCD Study. Title focal on smartphone ownership and health outcomes. Prospective associations examined.
Uses ABCD Study. Screen use examined as mediator of neurodevelopmental-psychiatric associations. Mediation analyses with statistics.
Uses ABCD Study. Smartphone ownership (age of acquisition) is focal outcome. Sociodemographic associations examined.
Uses ABCD Study. Recreational screen time is focal predictor of CVD risk outcomes. Regression analyses reported.
Uses ABCD Study. Gaming addiction is focal outcome variable. Tests psychosocial mediators of ADHD-gaming association.
PDF contains only supplementary methods and measurement descriptions (eMethods, eReferences) without analytical results section
Uses ABCD Study. Screen time is focal predictor of BMI/overweight-obesity. Prospective analyses with mutual adjustment.
Uses ABCD Study. Video game use is focal outcome variable. Media parenting practices are prospective predictors.

Uses ABCD Study data (baseline and 2-year follow-up). Problematic screen use is focal outcome variable. Tests statistical associations between PSU and mental health/sleep/substance use with extractable statistics (beta coefficients, p-values). Meets FT-IC1-4.

Uses ABCD Study data. Social media use trajectories are focal predictor variables. Tests statistical associations with spatial and verbal memory as outcomes, reporting extractable statistics. Meets FT-IC1-4.

Uses ABCD Study data. Social media use is focal predictor variable. Tests statistical associations with early adolescent brain structure, reporting effect sizes and statistical significance. Meets FT-IC1-4.

Uses ABCD Study data. Social media use is focal predictor variable. Tests statistical associations with smoking expectancies and nicotine experimentation, reporting extractable statistics. Meets FT-IC1-4.

Uses ABCD Study data. Dating app use (screen media type) is focal predictor variable. Tests statistical associations with depression symptoms as outcome, reporting extractable statistics. Meets FT-IC1-4.

Uses ABCD Study data (baseline Y0 ages 9-10, follow-up Y2 ages 11-12). Addictive screen use (composite of videogaming, social media, phone use) is focal outcome variable. Tests statistical associations with cortical maturation and reward activation, reporting beta coefficients with p-values and permutation tests (e.g., β=-0.056, p<.001 for max-ASU). Meets FT-IC1-4.

Uses ABCD Study data (years 2-3 follow-up). Problematic gaming (screen media type) is focal predictor variable. Tests statistical associations with psychotic-like experiences as outcome, reporting regression estimates with 95% CI. Meets FT-IC1-4.

Uses ABCD Study data (baseline rs-fMRI ages 9-10, PUSM at 4-year follow-up, N=2294). Social media problematic use is focal outcome variable. Tests statistical associations using connectome-based predictive modeling with 10-fold cross-validation, reporting correlation statistics (r=0.055, p=0.016, permutation p=0.02). Meets FT-IC1-4.
Uses ABCD data; screen media is focal variable in title/abstract; statistical associations tested
Uses ABCD data; screen media is focal variable in title/abstract; statistical associations tested
Uses ABCD data; screen media is focal variable in title/abstract; statistical associations tested
Uses ABCD data; screen media is focal variable in title/abstract; statistical associations tested
Uses ABCD data; screen media is focal variable in title/abstract; statistical associations tested
Uses ABCD data; screen media is focal variable in title/abstract; statistical associations tested
Uses ABCD data; screen media is focal variable in title/abstract; statistical associations tested
Uses ABCD data; screen media is focal variable in title/abstract; statistical associations tested

Psychometric/measurement study focused on validating the ABCD-EARS passive sensing tool for measuring smartphone use. Primary analyses examine measurement properties (reliability, validity, concordance between methods) rather than testing associations between screen use and health/behavioral outcomes.

Resource/methods documentation paper describing NowIKnowMyABCD, a centralized hub for ABCD researchers. Contains no statistical associations tested between screen media and outcomes. Descriptive/infrastructure only.

Primarily descriptive study examining longitudinal use patterns of technology subtypes across early adolescence. While it presents some comparative statistics and trajectories, the core analyses focus on describing use patterns by demographics and age rather than testing substantive associations between screen media and health/behavioral outcomes.

Uses ABCD Study data; screen time and problematic screen use are focal as predictors of eating disorder symptoms. Tests prospective associations via logistic regression (baseline screen time predicting year-two eating disorder outcomes). Reports extractable ORs, CIs, and p-values. Meets all inclusion criteria.

Uses ABCD Study data but screen media is not focal. Primary research question examines pet ownership (active vs. passive vs. none) and its association with physical activity/sports involvement. Screen time appears as secondary/exploratory outcome in hypothesis; focal variables are pet type and activity engagement, not screen media effects.

Psychometric/network analysis study examining the network structure of relationships between digital media types and psychotic-like experiences using network analysis methods. Primary focus is on mapping and validating network structure (centrality, bridge indices, network stability) rather than testing substantive causal associations.

Uses ABCD Study data; tests bidirectional associations between psychiatric symptoms and screen time (both social and nonsocial). Screen media is focal as both outcome and predictor. Reports logistic regression models with ORs, CIs, and p-values. Meets all inclusion criteria; demonstrates reciprocal relationships.

Uses ABCD Study data; examines causal relationships between screen use (TV, video, games, text, SNS, video chat) and brain development/cognition/behavior outcomes. Uses Mendelian randomization to test causal associations. Screen media is focal as predictor. Reports MR coefficients with CIs and p-values. Meets all inclusion criteria.

Uses ABCD Study data. Video game addiction (screen media outcome) is focal variable tested against reward processing predictor. Multiple statistical associations reported with p-values and confidence intervals. Meets all inclusion criteria.

Uses ABCD Study data. Screen time modalities (television, video games, videos, texting, social media) are focal predictor variables examined prospectively and cross-sectionally against nutrition outcomes. Multiple regression models with p-values and confidence intervals reported. Meets all inclusion criteria.

Uses ABCD Study data. Recreational screen use (television, video games, social media, mobile phones) examined as focal outcome variable in relation to transgender/gender-questioning identity. Multiple statistical associations reported with adjusted odds ratios and p-values. Meets all inclusion criteria.

Uses ABCD Study data. Bedtime screen use behaviors (TV, phone activities, video games, social media) are focal predictor variables examined prospectively against sleep outcomes. Multiple regression analyses with p-values and confidence intervals reported. Meets all inclusion criteria.

Uses ABCD Study data. Social media time is focal predictor variable examined prospectively against alcohol sipping outcome. Multiple regression models including mediator analysis with risk ratios, p-values, and confidence intervals reported. Meets all inclusion criteria.

Uses ABCD Study data. Screen time (continuous and categorical) is focal predictor variable examined against cardiovascular disease risk factors (blood pressure, cholesterol, hemoglobin A1c). Multiple linear regression analyses with p-values and confidence intervals reported. Meets all inclusion criteria.

Uses ABCD Study data. Screen time is focal predictor variable examined prospectively against mental health symptoms (depression, anxiety, conduct disorder, ADHD, somatic) using mixed-effects models. Multiple statistical associations with p-values reported. Meets all inclusion criteria.

Uses ABCD Study data. Online dating app use (screen-based digital behavior) is focal outcome variable examined across sociodemographic characteristics. Logistic regression analyses with adjusted odds ratios and p-values reported. Meets all inclusion criteria.
Perspective/commentary article discussing Carter et al.'s findings; no original data analysis or statistical testing of screen media associations
ABCD Study; social media use patterns are focal independent variables predicting youth mental health outcomes; exposure-wide analysis with extractable statistics (ORs, 95% CIs, p-values)

Primarily descriptive/null results study; digital media usage did not alter cortex or striatum development; only marginal social media-cerebellum finding with negligible effect (β=-0.03). Neuroimaging descriptive focus.

Screen media is not focal. Paper examines family environment (parental monitoring, family conflict) as predictors of cyberbullying victimization. Screen media only peripherally mentioned; cyberbullying is the focal outcome.

Screen media is not focal. Paper examines traumatic stress as predictor and deviant peer association as mediator of dual systems model outcomes (impulse control, sensation-seeking). Screen media has no direct analytic role.

ABCD Study; screen media activity is focal variable examined in cross-lagged panel models; bidirectional associations between sleep and SMA; extractable statistics (β values, 95% CIs, p-values). Screen media predicts externalizing symptoms.

ABCD Study; screen time is focal variable examined bidirectionally with sleep duration and internalizing symptoms; extractable statistics (ORs, 95% CIs). Screen time predicts depression/anxiety outcomes.

ABCD Study; screen time (streaming, gaming, socializing) is focal independent variable; examined via isotemporal substitution and compositional data analysis predicting BMI z-score; extractable statistics (β values, 95% CIs).

Uses ABCD Study data (FT-IC1: YES). Screen time (passive and social) is focal predictor variable (FT-IC2: YES). Tests statistical associations with obesity and autism status (FT-IC3: YES). Reports extractable statistics including regression coefficients and p-values (FT-IC4: YES).

Uses ABCD Study data longitudinally (FT-IC1: YES). Daily screen use is focal predictor of inhibitory control network outcomes (FT-IC2: YES). Tests statistical associations with fMRI inhibitory control networks (FT-IC3: YES). Reports extractable statistics from mixed models (FT-IC4: YES).

Uses ABCD Study prospective cohort data with participants ages 9-11 (FT-IC1: YES). Screen time is focal predictor of suicidal behaviors outcomes (FT-IC2: YES). Tests prospective statistical associations (FT-IC3: YES). Reports extractable statistics including hazard ratios, confidence intervals, and p-values (FT-IC4: YES).

Uses ABCD Study data (FT-IC1: YES), but screen media is NOT focal. Screen time is one component within an aggregated '24-hour movement' composite variable (alongside sleep and physical activity). Analytic focus is on the clustered movement construct, not on screen media as a focal predictor, outcome, mediator, or moderator. Screen media is treated as an integrated component of a broader lifestyle domain rather than a focal analytic variable.

Uses ABCD Study data (FT-IC1: YES), but screen media is NOT focal (FT-IC2: NO). Cyberbullying victimization is the focal predictor of substance initiation outcomes. Screen media (social media context) serves only as background context for cyberbullying exposure, not as a focal variable in its own analytic role. Screen media is peripheral to the study's primary question.

Uses ABCD Study data (FT-IC1: YES). Screen time is focal outcome variable affected by COVID-19 pandemic (FT-IC2: YES). Tests statistical associations between pandemic exposure and screen time outcomes (FT-IC3: YES). Reports extractable statistics including means, standard deviations, correlations, and p-values (FT-IC4: YES).

Uses ABCD Study data Release 3.0 (FT-IC1: YES), but screen media is NOT focal (FT-IC2: NO). Screen time is one of four lifestyle risk factors (sleep, physical activity, BMI) examined within an integrated latent change score model framework of 'clustered lifestyle risk factors.' Analytic focus is on the clustered lifestyle risk factor domain and its relationship to brain structure, not on screen media as a standalone focal variable. Screen media is a component variable in a composite construct.

Uses ABCD Study data as registered report (FT-IC1: YES). Digital screen media activity is focal predictor variable of functional brain organization (FT-IC2: YES). Tests statistical associations with resting-state fMRI network organization and brain connectivity (FT-IC3: YES). Reports extractable statistics including GFA factor loadings, network metrics, and statistical significance tests (FT-IC4: YES).

Uses ABCD Study data. Screen time is a focal exposure variable examined alongside physical activity. Logistic regression models estimate associations between screen time categories and BMI percentile/overweight-obesity outcomes with extractable RR and 95% CI.

Uses ABCD Study data but screen media is not focal. Cyberbullying victimization is the focal outcome predicted by adverse childhood experiences. Screen time is mentioned in keywords but is not analyzed as a primary variable or covariate in the models.
Uses ABCD Study data but screen media is not focal. Cyberbullying and sleep disturbance are the focal variables. Screen time is not a primary exposure or outcome variable in the statistical analyses.

Uses ABCD Study data. Screen time and problematic screen use (video games, social media, mobile phones) are focal outcome variables predicted by sexual orientation. Multiple linear regression analyses with extractable beta coefficients and 95% CI.

Uses ABCD Study data. Screen time is the focal exposure variable examined prospectively for association with obsessive-compulsive disorder. Logistic regression models with extractable adjusted odds ratios and 95% CI.

Uses ABCD Study data. Bedtime screen use behaviors and specific modalities (TV, video games, social media, messaging) are focal exposure variables examined for associations with sleep outcomes. Modified Poisson regression with extractable adjusted risk ratios and 95% CI.

Uses ABCD Study data. Contemporary screen time modalities (television, social media, video games, texting, video chat) are focal exposure variables examined prospectively for associations with conduct and oppositional defiant disorder. Modified Poisson regression with extractable prevalence ratios and 95% CI.

Uses ABCD Study data. Problematic social media use is the focal exposure variable examined for associations with positive and negative alcohol expectancies. Linear regression models with extractable beta coefficients and 95% CI.

Uses ABCD Study data. Confirmatory factor analysis on mental health constructs. Screen media not primary focus but included as part of comprehensive mental health measurement. Contains statistical associations and extractable statistics.
Opportunities/perspectives paper on transforming ABCD Study. Describes study design and future directions. No statistical associations tested; purely descriptive/commentary on ABCD cohort potential.
Examines family functioning typologies and 24-hour movement behaviors. Screen media appears as covariate only; analytic focus is on sleep/physical activity. Screen media not focal variable.

Uses ABCD Study data. Title explicitly identifies screen media activity as focal factor. Examines associations between extracurricular activities, screen media, and sleep with children's cognitive functioning. Reports extractable statistics (beta, p-values, correlations).

Uses ABCD Study data. Contemporary screen time modalities (weekday/weekend) examined as predictor of binge-eating disorder at 1-year follow-up. Reports statistical associations and extractable statistics (beta, OR, p-values).

Uses ABCD Study data. Contemporary screen time (weekday/weekend) examined as focal predictor of body mass index percentile at 1-year follow-up. Reports statistical associations with extractable statistics (beta, p-values).

Uses ABCD Study data. Screen time is focal predictor variable examined across multiple outcomes (mental health, academic performance, sleep, social outcomes). Extensive statistical results with beta coefficients, t-statistics, p-values separated by sex. Meets all inclusion criteria.

Psychometric/measurement study. Validates passive smartphone sensing (EARS app) against self-report. Focus is methodological: correlation between passive and self-reported screen use (r=0.49). No statistical associations between screen media and health/behavioral outcomes tested.

Uses ABCD data. Screen media is focal predictor of behavioral outcomes (internalizing/externalizing behaviors). Tests statistical associations with extractable statistics (multivariable models with p-values, confidence intervals).
Uses ABCD data. Screen time is focal variable examined across household income and race groups. Tests statistical associations with extractable statistics (comparisons, p-values).
Uses ABCD data but screen media is not a focal variable. Paper focuses on sleep disturbance predicting depression; screen media not discussed as a main predictor, outcome, mediator, or moderator.
Uses ABCD data but screen media is not focal. Paper examines correlates of BMI; screen media mentioned only minimally and not as primary variable of interest. Used as potential covariate only.

Uses ABCD data. Screen media is focal predictor of sleep disturbance symptom severity. Examines associations across multiple types of screen media (TV, video, gaming, social media, texting). Tests statistical associations with extractable statistics (odds ratios, confidence intervals, p-values in Tables 2-5).

Uses ABCD data. Screen media (weekend screen use time) is focal predictor of suicidality. Tests statistical associations with extractable statistics (OR 1.3, 95% CI 1.2-1.7, p<0.05). Identified as risk factor in logistic regression models.

Uses ABCD data. Screen media activity is focal predictor examining displacement hypothesis of other recreational activities. Tests statistical associations with extractable statistics (adjusted odds ratios with confidence intervals, p-values from logistic regression).

Uses ABCD data. Screen time is focal variable in network analysis examining associations with mood symptoms (depression, anhedonia, irritability, anger, elevated mood, decreased need for sleep). Tests statistical associations with extractable statistics (network edges with regression coefficients, node strength measures, p-values).
Does not use ABCD Study data. This is a Barcelona cohort study (n=2,233 children from Spain), not the US-based Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.

Uses ABCD Study data. Screen media (electronic media use) is a focal predictor variable examining associations with child anxiety and depression outcomes. Reports extractable statistics including regression coefficients and p-values.
Uses ABCD Study data. Screen time is focal as the primary predictor with sleep duration as mediator of problem behaviors outcome. Tests statistical mediation associations with extractable statistics.
Uses ABCD Study data. Screen time is focal as a predictor of impulsivity outcomes. Tests statistical associations with extractable statistics including odds ratios and p-values.
Does not use ABCD Study data. This is a European cohort study (Barcelona/Spain) examining radiofrequency and screen time in 5-year-olds, not the US-based ABCD Study.

Uses ABCD Study data (explicitly stated: 'from the ABCD study'). Screen media activity is focal predictor of brain structure outcomes. Tests statistical associations with extractable correlation statistics and brain region findings.

Uses ABCD Study data but this is a protocol/methods paper with no statistical associations tested. Describes planned measurement approaches for mobile technology and social media but contains no actual analyses or results.
Does not use ABCD Study data. This is a European cohort study (Barcelona/Spain) examining radiofrequency and cell phone use in 5-6 year-olds, not the US-based ABCD Study.

Uses ABCD data. Screen media (video games, social media, mobile phones) is focal variable tested as outcome. Reports extractable statistics: ORs (3.1x, 1.6x), adjusted/unadjusted models. Tests associations between ACEs and problematic screen use.
This is an editorial/introduction to a special issue on ABCD. No original data analysis. No statistical associations tested. Descriptive introduction to the ABCD cohort and its applications.

Uses ABCD data. Screen use trajectories are focal outcome variable. Tests associations between demographics/discrimination and screen use trajectories using growth mixture modeling. Reports extractable descriptive statistics and group comparisons (N values, percentages).

Uses ABCD data. Screen media activity (SMA) patterns are focal variable. Tests associations with behavioral and neuroimaging outcomes. Reports extractable statistics: beta coefficients, 95% CIs, FDR-corrected p-values (<.001).

Screen media is used only as a background covariate or characteristic, not as a focal variable. The study predicts ADHD symptoms from neurocognitive testing and youth characteristics; screen media has no analytic focus in the predictive model.

Uses ABCD Study data with screen time as the focal predictor variable. Tests statistical associations between screen time and psychiatric problems with extractable statistics (p-values, effect estimates). Meets all four inclusion criteria (FT-IC1 through FT-IC4).

Uses ABCD Study data but screen media is not a focal variable. Study examines associations between brain structural co-development and internalizing symptoms; screen media is not mentioned as a predictor, outcome, mediator, or moderator of interest.

Uses ABCD Study data with screen time as the focal predictor of internalizing disorder diagnoses. Tests statistical associations with extractable statistics (odds ratios, confidence intervals). Meets all four inclusion criteria.
Meets all inclusion criteria
Meets all inclusion criteria
Screen media not focal variable (only covariate or not present)
Screen media not focal variable (only covariate or not present)
Uses ABCD data. Screen time is focal DV. Tests association with mental health/resiliency factors via multiple linear regression. Reports beta, 95% CI, p-values.
No statistical association tested. Descriptive comparison of parent vs adolescent screen time reporting discrepancies; not testing screen time as predictor/outcome in analytic model.
Uses ABCD data. Screen time focal as IV predicting cyberbullying victimization/perpetration. Logistic regression with adjusted OR, 95% CI, p-values reported.
Uses ABCD data. Screen time use is focal DV. Tests association with sociodemographic factors via multiple linear regression. Reports beta, 95% CI, p-values.

Uses ABCD data. Gaming severity (VGAQ) is focal outcome. Tests statistical associations between brain connectivity and problematic gaming (r=0.12, p=0.002). Reports extractable stats: correlations, p-values, 95% CIs.

Uses ABCD data. Screen time is focal predictor of cognitive function. Tests multiple associations with all cognitive domains (Table 2 shows significant betas and FDR-corrected p-values). Extractable statistics: beta coefficients, 95% CIs, p-values.

Does not use screen media as a focal variable. This paper focuses on cyberbullying victimization, inhibitory control, and error processing. Screen media is not examined as a predictor, outcome, mediator, or moderator.

Uses ABCD data. Screen time across multiple activities (TV, video games, online chat, social media) is focal as bidirectional predictor/outcome. Tests temporal associations: TV-watching and social media predict increased irritability (edge weights 0.03, 0.12); bidirectional online chat-irritability link. Extractable statistics: edge weights, coefficients, 95% CIs.
Uses ABCD data; screen media is focal predictor/outcome; tests statistical associations; reports extractable statistics (beta/p-values)
Screen media used only as covariate/confounder, not focal variable
Uses ABCD data; screen media is focal predictor/outcome; tests statistical associations; reports extractable statistics (beta/p-values)
Uses ABCD data; screen media is focal predictor/outcome; tests statistical associations; reports extractable statistics (beta/p-values)

Uses ABCD Study data (release 5.1, n=4289 from Jan 2018-Dec 2022). Screen media (video gaming/gaming disorder) is the FOCAL outcome variable. Tests longitudinal associations between psychopathology and gaming disorder development using CLPMs across 4 time points with extractable beta coefficients, 95% CIs, and p-values (e.g., psychopathology beta=0.03 [95% CI, 0.002-0.06] at 2-3yr; beta=0.07 [0.04-0.10] at 3yr follow-up; p<.001).

Uses ABCD Study data (release 5.1, 3 waves ages 9-14). Screen use is a FOCAL mediator variable (tested in path models examining parental factors→sleep outcomes). Reports extractable statistics: screen use mediated parental monitoring→better sleep quality (indirect β=-0.004, 95% CI, 0.002-0.008, PM 73%; β=−0.003 for parental psychopathology path). Screen use also tested as mediator for family conflict associations.

Uses ABCD Study data (release 5.1, n=4,744 at baseline through year 4). Screen time and problematic screen use (PSU) are FOCAL outcome variables—entire analysis predicts ST and PSU by baseline individual differences. Tests associations across 4 domains (impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, cognition) with extractable betas for video games (e.g., urgency β=+.07 [SE=.01, p<.001]), social media (β=+.06 [.01, p<.001]), and PSU outcomes with specific cutoffs and statistics.

Uses ABCD dataset (n=9,337 baseline with white matter MRI data). Social media consumption is the FOCAL predictor variable. Tests associations with white matter fractional anisotropy (FA) in 34 tracts using linear regressions. Reports extractable odds ratios for social media use (female, black race, lower income, puberty: OR>1.5, p<0.05) and adjusted linear regressions for FA (initially significant but not after controlling for race/SES; provides sample sizes and p-values).

Uses ABCD data (N=3037, ages 11-12). Screen time is focal variable tested as predictor of brain architecture. Reports extractable statistics (r=0.13, p=2.6×10^-19 for overall screen time association with network segregation/integration; multiple beta values for screen time-outcome associations).

Uses ABCD data (N=6554, ages 9-13). Social media use trajectories are focal predictor variable. Tests associations with cognitive performance using linear regression. Reports extractable statistics (e.g., B=-1.39, 95% CI -2.10 to -0.68 for ORRT; B=-2.03, 95% CI -2.91 to -1.15 for PSMT).

Uses ABCD data (N=11,876, ages 9-10 baseline). Social media time (hours/day) is focal predictor variable. Tests prospective associations with depressive symptoms across 4 waves using cross-lagged panel models. Reports extractable statistics (β=0.07, 95% CI 0.01-0.12, p=.01 for year 1→2; β=0.09, 95% CI 0.04-0.14, p<.001 for year 2→3).

Uses ABCD data (N=9,243, ages 10-11). Screen time (overall and 6 subtypes: social media, texting, videos, video games, television, video chat) is focal predictor variable. Tests prospective associations with manic symptoms (7 Up subscale). Reports extractable statistics (B=0.05 95% CI 0.03-0.07, p<0.001 for overall; B=0.20 95% CI 0.09-0.32, p=0.001 for social media; B=0.18 95% CI 0.08-0.28, p<0.001 for texting).

Screen media is not focal. This paper examines cyberbullying victimization as the outcome with gender identity as the exposure. Digital media is discussed contextually as a platform where bullying occurs, but screen media use is not an analytic focus of the paper.

No statistical association tested. Paper is purely descriptive, documenting prevalence and patterns of social media use in early adolescents without testing associations between screen media and outcomes.

Uses ABCD data; screen media (social media, texting, video chatting) is focal predictor; tests prospective associations with substance use experimentation; reports extractable statistics (ORs 1.18-1.20, CIs, p<0.001).

Uses ABCD data; social media time is focal independent variable; tests prospective association with cannabis experimentation; tests cannabis expectancies as mediator; reports extractable statistics (B=0.18, 95% CI 0.13-0.24, indirect effects mediated 19.8% and 13.6%).

Cyberbullying is the focal exposure variable, not screen media. While cyberbullying occurs through social media/screens, screen media characteristics are not examined as a focal variable (predictor, outcome, mediator, or moderator). Screen media is an implicit mechanism of cyberbullying but not a focal analytic variable.

Uses ABCD Study data (Year 3). Bedtime screen use is a focal exposure variable examined for prospective associations with sleep outcomes. Tests statistical associations with extractable statistics (regression coefficients, CIs, p-values). Meets all 4 inclusion criteria.

Uses ABCD Study data (Year 3). Adolescent screen time and problematic screen use (social media, video games, mobile phones) are focal outcome variables. Tests statistical associations between media parenting practices and screen outcomes with extractable statistics (regression coefficients, CIs, p-values). Meets all 4 inclusion criteria.

Cyberbullying victimization is the focal exposure, manic symptoms are the focal outcome. Screen media (total screen time) is included only as a covariate in adjusted models, not as a focal variable. Does not meet FT-IC2 criterion (screen media as focal variable).
Uses ABCD Study data. Screen media (R-rated movies, mature-rated video games) is focal variable. Tests associations between parenting practices and media consumption with extractable statistics.

While uses ABCD data and tests longitudinal changes, screen time appears to be examined as one of multiple co-equal descriptive outcomes (alongside sleep and sports/exercise) without analytic focus on screen media as predictor, outcome, mediator, or moderator in a specific statistical model.
Uses ABCD Study data. Gaming is focal variable in mediation analysis with caffeine as mediator and sleep as outcome. Tests statistical association with extractable mediation results.
Uses ABCD Study data. Screen time is focal predictor variable. Tests associations with anxiety/depression and alcohol/e-cigarette use outcomes with extractable statistics.
Screen use mentioned only as lifestyle covariate affecting intelligence development, not as focal analytic variable. Primary focus is on crystallized vs. fluid intelligence neural mechanisms.

ABCD data used; television viewing is primary independent variable with direct statistical associations tested against neurocognitive performance and brain morphology; extractable beta, p-values, effect sizes reported throughout.
ABCD data used; problematic social media use is primary independent variable; tests associations with emotional/behavioral dysregulation and moderation effects; reports beta, 95% CIs, p-values.

ABCD data used; digital media use (social media, gaming, television) is primary predictor of ADHD symptoms; tests longitudinal associations over 4 years; reports standardized beta coefficients, p-values, cumulative effect sizes.

Uses ABCD Study data. Social media addiction (SMA) scores are focal predictor of binge eating across concurrent and prospective analyses (Poisson regression). Extractable statistics: prevalence ratios, risk ratios with 95% CI, p-values. Meets FT-IC1-IC4.

Uses ABCD Study data. Screen time is focal independent variable tested for association with ADHD symptoms (linear mixed-effects models, β=0.032, p=0.001). Tests mediation via brain structure. Extractable statistics: beta coefficients, 95% CI, p-values, effect sizes (d). Meets FT-IC1-IC4.

Uses ABCD Study data. Addictive use trajectories (social media, mobile phones, video games) are focal predictors of suicidal behaviors/ideation and mental health over 4-year follow-up. Poisson/GLM regression with multiple statistically significant associations (e.g., social media RR=2.14-2.39 for suicidal behaviors). Extractable statistics: risk ratios, 95% CI, T-score differences, p-values. Meets FT-IC1-IC4.

Uses ABCD Study data and tests both VGA and SMA. However, SMA shows NO significant longitudinal associations with reward or loss sensitivity (abstract states 'SMA showed no significant longitudinal associations'). While VGA is focal with significant VGA×Time interactions, SMA is a covariate-like variable without meaningful analytic findings. Since social media is one of the two focal screen media variables and it lacks statistical associations/meaningful results, screen media is not meaningfully focal.

Uses ABCD data (Release 5.1). Screen media (problematic video game, social media, mobile phone use) is FOCAL variable in network analysis examining associations with behavioral/environmental outcomes. Multiple statistical associations tested with extractable statistics (cross-lagged panel-network analyses, out-strength values reported).

Uses ABCD data (Release 5.0). Screen media (mature-rated video games, R-rated movies) is FOCAL outcome variable predicted by sleep and sensation-seeking. Multiple statistical associations tested with extractable statistics (prospective associations reported with p-values, interaction effects, bidirectional associations).

Uses ABCD data. Paper is a PSYCHOMETRIC/MEASUREMENT study examining discrepancies between self-reported and objective screen time measurement (EARS app vs self-report). Focus is on measurement validity and reliability, not on testing substantive associations involving screen media as predictor/outcome/mediator/moderator. This is measurement validation only.

Uses ABCD data (Release 4.0). Screen time is FOCAL outcome variable predicted by parental monitoring and family conflict. Multiple statistical associations tested with extractable statistics (linear regression: parental monitoring B = -0.37; family conflict B = 0.08, with 95% CIs reported). Prospective design across 6 screen modalities.
